english: mentioned in the above passages were 302 and 1002, and not 322 and 1022. — 3. In bcù-rtsa nyí-ṡu-rtsa (bcu rtsa nyi shu rtsa), brgyá-rtsa (brgya rtsa), without any units following, e.g Tar. 120, 10, the word evidently stands but pleonastically, like tam-pa (tam pa).

(Bal., Pur. rtswa (rtswa), stswa (stswa)) C. *tsa*, Lh., Ld., *sa*, तृण, grass, herb, plant, rtsa-kʻai (rtsa kha'i) (or rtsa-rtsei (rtsa rtse'i)) zil-pa (zil pa) the dew on the grass Glr.; rtsa nyag ɣcig (rtsa nyag gcig) a single blade of grass Cs.; ṡṅó-yi rtsa (shngo yi rtsa), rtsa-ṡṅón (rtsa shngon) green grass; rtsa-skám (rtsa skam), and often rtsa (rtsa) alone, hay, rtsa rṅá-ba (rtsa rnga ba) to mow grass, ꜝtʻú-ba ('thu ba), to gather (grass); rtsá-kʻa (rtsa kha) C., W. pasture, pasturage, *sá-kʻa gyál-la* W. good pasturage. — rtsá-ċan (rtsa can) covered with grass, grassy. — rtsa-mčóg (rtsa mchog) Kusha-grass Lex., v. ku-ṡa (ku sha); rtsa-mčoy(-groṅ) (rtsa mchoy grong) town in West Assam, where Buddha died Glr.; Kamarūpa. — rtsa-tʻág (rtsa thag) grass-rope Dzl. — rtsa-tʻún (rtsa thun) grass-gatherer Sch. — rtsa-ɣdán (rtsa gdan) grass-mat Sch. — rtsa-ɣyáb (rtsa g-yab) manger Sch. — rtsa-ras (rtsa ras) Sch. 'linen', prop. the same as la-ta (la ta) q. v. — 2. euphemism for rkyag (rkyag); *tsa táṅ-wa* C. to go to stool; rtsa ču bsdams (rtsa chu bsdams) Mil. he suffers from obstruction and strangury.

1. cf. rtsan (rtsan) and rtsad (rtsad), Ssk. मूल, 1. root (W. com. *bá-tʻag* for it), stalk of fruits; rtsá-ba lṅa (rtsa ba lnga) five (medicinal) roots, viz. rá-mnye (ra mnye), lčá-ba (lca ba), nyé-ṡiṅ (nye shing), a-ṡo (a sho) (better á-ṡa (a sha))-gandha, ɣzé-ma (gze ma); rtsá-ba-nas ꜝbyín-pa (rtsa ba nas 'byin pa) etc. to pull out with the root, to eradicate, extirpate, mostly fig., v. below. — 2. the lower end of a stick, trunk of a tree, pillar; má-kʻog rtsá-ba (ma khog rtsa ba) id. Mil.; the foot of a hill, mountain-pass, the latter also lá-rtsa (la rtsa) W. *lár-sa*; rtsá-bai žal (rtsa ba'i zhal), lag (lag) the lower faces or hands of those images, that represent deities with many faces and hands Glr.; rtsá-bai ṅos (rtsa ba'i ngos) base of a triangle Tar. 204, 1; fundament, foundation-pillar, and the like; in later literature and vulgo rtsá-bar (rtsa bar) and rtsar (rtsar), rarely (Glr.) rtsá-ru (rtsa ru) postp. with genit., to, at, e.g. to go to, to come to, to be at, both of persons and things, bud-méd-kyi rtsar nyál-ba (bud med kyi rtsar nyal ba) or more euphem., pʻyín-pa (phyin pa) to go to a woman Glr., ṡiṅ-gi rtsar (shing gi rtsar), even čui rtsar (chu'i rtsar) Glr.; at, near, to, a tree, river etc.; so also rtsá-la (rtsa la) to, at; rtsá(-ba)-na (rtsa ba na) Glr. and vulgo (incorr.) *tsá-ne* C. at, near; without a case following: rtsar byúṅ-nas (rtsar byung nas) coming near, stepping up to Glr. — 3. root fig. origin, primary cause, also ɣži-rtsa (gzhi rtsa), e.g. ꜝkʻór-bai ɣži-rtsa ɣcód-pa ('khor ba'i gzhi rtsa gcod pa) Mil. to cut off the root of transmigration, to deliver a soul from tr.; rtsá-ba-nas ꜝbyín-pa (rtsa ba nas 'byin pa), ꜝdón-pa ('don pa), ꜝgóg-pa ('gog pa) etc., also tsáṅ-nas (tsang nas), tsád-nas ɣcód-pa (tsad nas gcod pa) etc., to exterminate (root and branch), to annihilate; on the other hand: rtsa-brdár-ɣcód-pa (rtsa brdar gcod pa) Mil., rtsád-ɣcód-pa (rtsad gcod pa) to examine closely, to investigate thoroughly. — nyon-móṅs-kyi rtsá-ba ɣsum (nyon mongs kyi rtsa ba gsum) are the three primary moral evils, viz. ꜝdod-čágs ('dod chags), že-sdaṅ (zhe sdang), ɣtí-mug (gti mug); rtsa-brál (rtsa bral) therefore might signify: he who has freed himself from them; but it seems to mean also: without beginning or end, unlimited, e.g. snyíṅ-rǰe (snying rje) Glr., sems-nyíd (sems nyid) Mil.; dgé-bai rtsá-ba (dge ba'i rtsa ba), dgé-rtsa (dge rtsa) a virtuous deed, as a cause of future reward, skyéd-pa (skyed pa), spyód-pa (spyod pa), byéd-pa (byed pa) to perform such a deed; rtsá-bai (rtsa ba'i) ... the original, primary, principal ... e.g. don (don), primitive or first meaning Cs.; rtsá-bai nyon-móṅs-pa (rtsa ba'i nyon mongs pa) Cs.: 'original sin', Sch.: 'sin inherited from former births'; at all events not identical with the original sin of Christian dogmatics, although the word grammatically might denote it; rtsa(-bai) rgyud (rtsa ba'i rgyud) an introductory treatise, giving a summary of the contents of a larger work, e.g. of the rgyud-bži (rgyud bzhi), mentioned sub brgyud (brgyud); also title of other works, Ssk. मूलतन्त्र, v. Cs. Gram., chronol. table; whether Sch.'s translation 'cause and effect' is altogether correct, may admit of some doubt, yet v. below; rtsá-ba daṅ ꜝgrél-ba (rtsa ba dang 'grel ba) Cs. 'text and commentary'; in rtsá-bai ma (rtsa ba'i ma) Thgy. the genitive case stands prob. for the apposition: the mother that is the root of me, in a similar manner as rtsá-bai raṅ-bžín (rtsa ba'i rang bzhin) nature Cs.; rtsá-bai blá-ma (rtsa ba'i bla ma) seems to denote the teaching priest, the one by whom in any particular case the instruction is given, opp. to brgyúd-pa (brgyud pa), he to whom it is imparted. A good deal of confusion however prevails here, owing to the ambiguity of the verbal form in brgyúd-pa (brgyud pa) and the variable spelling; <
